story:
My brother Ted had a gift for creative mischief. He could mimic voices, reenact everyday scenes until we were breathless with laughter, and somehow cheer the overlooked and lonely. His energy ran hot and bright, daring everyone to keep up. He and I were best friends—he was born on Dad’s birthday, I on Mom’s two years later—and tucked in the middle of six other siblings, we liked the noise and the crowd. Pets multiplied as quickly as children in our house, but Ted’s white mouse with pink eyes and a ratty tail—Ent—stole the show.

Ent’s official home was an old aquarium on the boys’ dresser, but most days he scurried along baseboards, pink claws ticking on tile, startling babysitters by darting across the living room and vaulting over polished shoes. Often as not, he rode in Ted’s shirt or jeans pocket, his twitchy nose the only sign of stowaway status. Adults rarely noticed.

We weren’t bookworms, but we devoured The Guinness Book of World Records. We giggled at the longest fingernails and the stretchiest skin and plotted how we, too, might make the cut. After fierce debate, we settled on Ent as our path to fame. Ted built a tiny sulky with plywood wheels. Mom, champion of experiments, sewed a miniature harness for my rabbit, Ebony. We buckled Ebony in, latched Ent into his carriage, and let fly. The rabbit detonated across the yard, the sulky bounced, the harness gave way, and down the bank went wagon and mouse. Heart in throat, we unlatched the door. Ent emerged unruffled, whiskers quivering, toes and nails all accounted for. Back into Ted’s pocket he went.

Next we planned a trip to “space.” From our chaotic entryway—littered with reels, busted sticks, string nests, and the carcass of a giant box kite—we fished out a red nylon survivor. Ted poked breathing holes in an empty cottage cheese container, I added a slice of cheese for in-flight service, and we tied the capsule a few inches below the kite with dental floss. I held the kite high; Ted ran full tilt into the wind. It caught, soared, and the white container bobbed smaller and smaller until it vanished, the kite a speck in a blue infinity. We chattered about the moon, but we also worried. The line stayed taut. Ent stayed aloft till dusk. Bringing him down was a masterclass, everyone chiming in with advice from under the branches that already looked like a kite graveyard. At last, Ent disembarked—nose twitching, toes intact—ready for more.

April Fools’ Day loomed large at school. We planned to tuck Ent into the shallow front drawer of Ted’s beloved teacher Sharon’s desk, imagining her laugh when she reached for a red pen. The previous year, Ted had dropped rinsed earthworms into milk cups as a prank—a reckless stunt no one allowed him to repeat once he grasped how gross and potentially unsafe it was. This year, before the first bell, I went with Ted to his classroom, set Ent gently inside with room to breathe, and then hurried off to my own room down the hall. Sharon taught all morning without sitting. By recess, I had slipped back to Ted’s doorway and found him sick with worry about Ent. At last, near lunch, she eased into her chair and slid open the drawer.

A scream split the room. Her chair crashed back, and her legs flew up like startled saplings. Ted bolted to help. Ent peeked over the pens, whiskers smug. Classmates fluttered to lift Sharon; a messenger ran for the principal. Sharon steadied herself, breathed, and, because she understood ten-year-olds, looked past the fright to the hearts behind it. She knew Ted’s pranks weren’t mean-spirited, and she believed good behavior doesn’t guarantee a good heart—nor bad behavior a bad one. We learned to pair fun with care.

We never wrote to Guinness. But with Ent the Mouse, we had our share of record-worthy adventures—and a lot of joy.
